speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation, date is July 2nd 1996. Survivor is Sally Huppert, born Peller. Interviewer is Dina Cohen. The interview is in New York, New York, USA. And the language will be English. My name is Dina Cohen. Today is July 2nd, 1996. I am conducting an interview with Sally Huppert, born Peller, in New York, New York, USA. The interview will be in English. Could you please state your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sally Huppert, Sally, S-A-L-L-Y H-U-P-P-E-R-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and the name that you had at birth was?

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth is Sara Peller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sara Peller, S-A-R-A P-E-L-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Alla.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could just spell that too?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-L-L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- I'm born in 1910.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date, December 6.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of your birth was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stroza.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-R-O-Z-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and that country-- and that was in what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And do you remember the town of Stroza?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's a small town, and just at the trains was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lot of trains changed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Nowa [? Sarzyna. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live in the town? Did you-- what kind of a home did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My families was-- I was the oldest, Sally. Now it's Rega, younger one, Necha, Hilda, the youngest, Hilda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you close with your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. Blanka, I forgot Blanka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of things did you do with your sisters growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fighting. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to school in Stroza?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I go in Stroza, and later we moved to Grybow, I went to-- in Grybow in school to cheder. I went in school at Grybow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you just went to cheder, or did you go to a public school as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go, go to a public school, yes. I go to Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] things did you learn in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there both Jews and non-Jews in your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was a girls school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have contact with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have friends, very good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience any antisemitism as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do? How did he support the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't support. He, he is-- he came from the army. And he was in Italy. He was-- he came sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: The army from World War I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, how did your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had uncle in Germany. He support us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of friends? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My house was always a lot of people, a lot of children. And my girlfriends, they came to us, and they stay with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of things did you do with, with your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We read. We learn a lot. We didn't have no television. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a large extended family nearby, aunts, uncles, grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nearby, no. I used to go every vacation, I used to go to my grandparents in nice-- their-- a large family. There was a large family, uncles and aunts, and I visit them, just a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mszana Dolna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you spent the summers with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always the summers with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your entire family go or-- when you went for the summers, was it just you, or did your family go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my family didn't go with me. I was just there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- what kinds of things did you do on that farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: On that farm we visit our aunts. We go for trips, in neighborhood, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's all, not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that you were born in Stroza. And then you moved to Grybow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you move anywhere else after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, we moved to Rabka.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you move to Rabka? Do you remember how old you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh maybe-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was young. I don't remember the date.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what kind of a town was Rabka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabka was a beautiful town. Rabka is-- was a lot of people came for this, with children for baths, for massages, for inhalations, and always music. And I used to love to go to listen to music.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it, Rabka? What big town was it near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabka, was near Zakopane.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a lot of friends in Rabka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have friends. I have customers. I had a corset factory. I-- not factory. No factory, only corsetiere. I make girdles and brassieres for the customers what they came to Rabka, and I make money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you started working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very young. But I was-- before, I was working in by a lawyer. I learned to type there. And I went to the refinery, refinery. Petroleum refinery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, refinery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Refinery. I was their secretary. And I took care of all the expediency of all the workers. I make a lot of money there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that in Rabka also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Stroza. This was not far from Rabka. I traveled from Grybow to Stroza to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once you started working, did you have a lot of contact with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever experience any antisemitism at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were very nice to me. Maybe they've-- because they need me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you aware of events in Germany in the early '30s--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as Hitler was rising to power.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you and your family react to those events?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very nervous for it. We worry about us. But we couldn't help it. I remember my uncle came from-- I had two uncles in Germany. And each have factories. Stockings, they make stockings. They colored them. And they were German, in Germany, with children. And we find out about. And they, they went to Swiebodzin. They send them away from their house. They were eating supper and the Germans came and sent them away to the [? Swiebodzin. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And they sent them away to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To [? Swiebodzin. ?] And they kill-- and from there they, they want to kill them. But I remember my uncle, I don't know if they want to kill him. I don't know. They were very disappointed at the Germans. And they came to-- went to Poland, and they stay in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your uncles escaped from Germany to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's how you learned of that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did your family ever think of leaving Europe in those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but they couldn't afford it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, did they take any actions about leaving or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. OK. And what do you remember about the German invasion of Poland in September of '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is a long story and a hard story. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] But I have to tell you. They came, [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] we find out the Gestapo was going to be there in, in this Rabka. We were very sad, sad about it. But we couldn't help it. They took our children, my sisters. They took to Tereska. Tereska was a place where they say-- where they-- where they had the headquarters, headquarters. And all the children, the girls, they have to clean for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the Gestapo come in right in September, or when did they come?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent from the Judenrat. Because they went to the Judenrat. They need girls to clean. And the, the Judenrat sent us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did Germans occupy Rabka immediately after the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and they established a Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any members of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that they took girls to the Gestapo headquarters to work. Were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not taken, because I was breadwinner. And I worked for the German girls. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But later they took everything, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you worked for the German women, making undergarments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, making-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go into their homes or did they come to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to come to me. But I went to-- saw some of [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], that's a woman who take care of the children. She take care of the children. So she ask me I should take off the arm band. I have this. Because she doesn't want that the children should know. I did take off-- in there, it was before the holidays. I went there, and I-- she had a friend, Rosenbaum also her friend. He came there visiting her. And I saw him, he saw me. You know, I was without the arm band.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, [NON-ENGLISH] arm band? And she explained him I-- she asked me about it. And he was-- he didn't want to listen. He, he say, throw me out. And I went out. And he went after me with the gun. He wanted to shoot me. I hide under the stairs. I don't I didn't-- he didn't see me. And I was lucky I-- he didn't kill me. Do you remember when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was Rosenbaum?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosenbaum was the head of the Gestapo. And he, he killed people. He-- I must tell you. It was a family there, and they're Jews. And they have two children. And Rosenbaum is-- with the name of Rosenbaum. And he said they have to be killed. In a Friday night, he sent for them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They have a boy. He was working. And they sent for the boy. And the boy came home, and they want to take him. They didn't take him. The boy asked, should light candles. And later they came, the Gestapo, and they kill all three of them. All four of them, the mother, the father and two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know why they were killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they have the same name what he-- he has, he had. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you learn that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We find out from the people, what they're working in the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your sisters work in the Gestapo headquarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, every day my mother was worrying, every day they working, they, they clean the windows, Gestapo man-- oh, what it his name? Another one, it was a higher one. He was the one. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your parents ever taken to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- had papers. He was invalid. And I bring the papers to the-- to-- what is his name? I don't remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: To a German official?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to the head there. And I tell him he's-- he took the papers. And he didn't send for my father. But my father moved away, ran away from Rabka. He went to his sister to [NON-ENGLISH]. And they killed him there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you know that, that he was killed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- all the-- all the Polacks [NON-ENGLISH]. And they say, [NON-ENGLISH], all the Jew they took to Bobowa. And they killed in Bobowa all the Jews. They make a mass grave, a mass grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bobowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, do you know when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't know when.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you say the Polacks took them, or the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So, did you remain in Rabka with your mother and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remained in Rabka with my mother and sisters. My sisters were working in Gestapo. And I was working also. And we wait till that one evening, they came, tomorrow, we have to-- people-- my mother has to go. They killed them all. So I said to my mother, why you should go away? We ran away. All of us ran away from Rabka. And they were looking for us. They couldn't find us, because we, we didn't went together. Everybody went in different direction. I had a sister. She's still-- I don't know what happened to her. She-- they probably killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which sister was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Necha.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they came, to tell you that they were going to take you away, was there a ghetto in Rabka? Or was that just your home that they came?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my home, but a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe the ghetto in Rabka?

speaker: interviewee
text: They close up the street. That's all. We have to go out from 3:00 to-- no, from 3:00 to 4:00, or 4:00 to 5:00. And if we need something we have to go out in this time, just small city. No, this a-- this was a country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was beautiful there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how-- was-- the town was beautiful you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the ghetto? What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- was there barbed wire or guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no wire. No wire. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We never went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who came to say that you had to be taken away? You said someone came--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Gestapo-- no, I don't know from, the Gestapo or from the Judenrat, I don't know. I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We know about-- they were talking about all the children, hear about this in the Gestapo. They were working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you run to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a forest near, near some boy, who bring us milk, and a girl came-- came just in the same day. She came, and she said to my mother, you can come in my house. I live alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother came in her house, with my sister, and the, the youngest, Hilda, came her house. So, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she said that she can stay in her house. And later, she throw away-- throw out my mother. Because she find out they are going to kill her, if she's going to keep Jews. So she throw my mother out. And I make up with my mother. I have to pick, I'm going to pick her up, and we're going to go to where all, all the Jews are going. I wanted to go to Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I'm not-- I didn't know where to look for her. Since she throw away, she said, she's gone somewhere else. And I didn't know where to look for her. And I didn't see her till then, till today. I know they-- a woman came. I went to my woman, what she-- I was working for her. And she was a very good-- a good friend. And she said, I know your mother was killed. In the same day, she tell me, your mother was-- your sister was killed, and your mother was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a Polish woman, or a German?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Polish woman, a good woman. She came to my house, and she said, Sally, you can stay at my house. And she keep me in the-- in the attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in that attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah. Later I went from her house, I went to Cracow

speaker: interviewer
text: And had--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sosnowiec. Because I have a friend in Warsaw. And she said when something happen, come to Sosnowiec. I take you to Warsaw, a Polish girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get there from Rabka? How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from Rabka, a run by legs through forest. I came to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to Cracow, I didn't go. I went to the train. Now I remember. I went to the train. And I don't know how they didn't see me. But they have black uniforms, the Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any identification?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I make believe I sleep. It was a sitting car, old women around me. So I beg her she let me sleep on their arm. They didn't wake me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you speak with anyone, or did anyone speak with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't speak with no one on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you say you went from Rabka to Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: By train?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. And when I came to Sosnowiec, they-- I was very dizzy. I went to a cafeteria there by the train, and I did wrong. And the man was nice, and said, go out. I should go out. He said, look out. They hang-- I don't know how many-- I don't remember how many. They hang the Jew's head there. You don't want to hang there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you did wrong. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to cafeteria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were you wearing your star or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't wear a star. But my face said.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sosnowiec, I stay mine girlfriend's friends overnight, and I left. I couldn't wait. And she had broken her wrist. She was in Warsaw. She was not in Sosnowiec. This was her friends. They tell me, look. You have to wait maybe a couple weeks. Said I couldn't do that. Because I didn't know them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- back to Cracow to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get from Sosnowiec to Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I say whatever will be will be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long a trip is that, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know people in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I came to Cracow to ghetto, to the ghetto. And there was standing a friend, what used to live in our building. And he said, you're going to sister? I said, where are my sisters? Rega was in Cracow. So perfect. I went to my sister.
